sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: A 60-minute workshop introducing inflation, price level changes, and simple measures used in introductory economics.

content.statement:
By the end of this session you will be able to:
- explain what inflation means in everyday and economic terms
- compare CPI and GDP deflator at an introductory level
- interpret a simple inflation scenario for different households

sections.heading: Key ideas
sections.content: Inflation is a sustained rise in the general price level. CPI and the GDP deflator are common introductory measures with different coverage.

content.title: Inflation concepts warm-up
content.purpose: Activate prior knowledge and agree a working definition of inflation.
content.learner_task: Discuss in your group and complete the task cards.
content.expected_output: A shared definition plus evidence from scenarios.

content.title: Household scenario — fixed income
content.learner_task: Read all scenarios and complete the worksheet table.
content.expected_output: Completed worksheet rows for each scenario.

content.title: Household scenario — variable costs
content.learner_task: Compare impacts across scenarios using the worksheet.
content.expected_output: A short comparison statement supported by table evidence.

content.title: Policy and communication wrap-up
content.learner_task: Use the discussion prompts to debrief as a class.
content.expected_output: Two contributions per group to the whole-class discussion.

content.content:
## Inflation Scenarios

### Scenario A: Grocery prices
Bread rises from £1.20 to £1.50 over a year while wages are unchanged.

### Scenario B: Rent and energy
Rent is fixed but energy bills increase sharply for a student household.

### Card 1 — Define inflation
Write a one-sentence definition of inflation in your own words.

### Card 2 — Spot the price change
List three prices that increased and one that stayed the same.

### Card 3 — Who is affected?
Name one winner and one loser from the scenarios.

### Card 4 — Everyday language
Rewrite your definition without jargon.

### Card 5 — Evidence check
Quote one number from a scenario to support your definition.

### Card 6 — Group agreement
Agree a final definition with your group.

| Item | Rising price? | Affects students? |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Bread |  |  |
| Rent |  |  |
| Energy |  |  |

| Measure | What it tracks | Limitation |
| --- | --- | --- |
| CPI | Consumer basket prices | Basket may not match your spending |
| PPI | Producer input prices | Not a direct cost-of-living measure |
| GDP deflator | All domestically produced goods | Less intuitive for learners |

content.heading: Discussion prompts
prompts: Which measure would you report to a student newspaper?
Who might be harmed most by the scenarios?
What policy response is hinted at in the overview?

sections.heading: Before you submit
items: ☐ Table has at least two completed rows

content.content:
## Household impacts

### Scenario A: Pensioner on fixed income
Most spending is on food and heating; income does not rise with prices.

### Scenario B: Graduate with student loan
Rent is fixed but transport and food costs rise faster than wages.

### Scenario C: Dual-income family
Mortgage is fixed but childcare and groceries rise quickly.

### Scenario D: Casual worker
Hours are cut while prices rise on essentials.

| Scenario | Who is affected? | Main price pressure | Policy angle |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  |  |  |  |

### Scenario 1: Young renter
Energy and food bills rise; rent is unchanged this year.

### Scenario 2: Small shop owner
Input costs rise but customers resist higher shelf prices.

### Scenario 3: Exporter
Domestic costs rise while foreign demand is weak.

| Scenario | Cost pressure | Revenue pressure | Short-run response |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  |  |  |  |

## Discussion prompts
- Name one group helped and one group harmed by recent inflation.
- Which measure (CPI or GDP deflator) would you cite in a news headline?
- What is one limitation of your chosen measure?

content.learner_actions: Discuss scenarios and complete task cards.

items.stem: Which situation best illustrates inflation rather than a one-off price change?
options: A general rise in many prices over time
A fall in quality of one good
